transcript.text: Nice to meet you.
This meeting is being recorded.
Hope you had a good time in Houston.
You know, I mean, this week was a little bit hectic, but yeah, I like coming down.
Definitely.
Have you been to any good barbecue spots outside of Papato's?
I have.
I've been to Truth, and I've been to Pit Room, and I've actually been to Corkscrew, if you've been there.
Corkscrew is in...
Old Town Spring.
Yeah, Spring, right.
Yeah, exactly.
Yeah, I grew up in Tomball, so that's not too far from where my parents live, and I actually had Corkscrew for my birthday last year.
Do really?
I mean, they got the Michelin star, so you got to try it.
Well, they do, but so I was down there in June, and then my son, who just graduated high school this year, had nothing really particular going on.
So I called him up.
It was like a Tuesday morning or something, and I called him up, and I said, Dude, why don't fly down here?
We have...
We're...
We're...
We're...
We'll have some barbecue together.
So he flew down Tuesday afternoon and we went to a couple of places on Tuesday.
No, actually, we only went to one.
We went to J&B, what's it called?
J&M Bar or something like that.
They were fine.
were good ribs.
But then Wednesday morning, we got up early and went to Corkscrew and lined up, you know.
So we were like number five in line.
And, I mean, it was amazing.
The place is great.
So, you know, that's great.
I feel like Austin has like better barbecue than Houston, but any good city in Texas is going to deliver.
It's going to be a matter of taste, right?
So I've figured out, you know, because I used to do, because I cook a lot.
So I used to do like a lot of seasoning and stuff.
But after I went to Corkscrew, I realized salt and pepper and garlic, you know, and paprika.
Thank you,
Maybe a little chili powder or something.
And that's it, right?
Like no sugar, none of that.
And then they smoke it with red oak.
So I went home and I started doing that.
So I've been spending the last three months with my son, you know, since he's a foodie as well.
So we've been like cooking pork ribs here all summer and try to get them as close to corkscrew as possible.
I'm getting damn close, dude.
Like I'm not far up.
That's incredible.
I love to hear that.
Well, as much as we'd both love to keep talking about food, I know that Yusuf introduced you and sounds like you have a little bit of context around what we do.
But maybe we can just take it off.
Yeah.
Why don't you give me like the, just give me the two minute pitch because then I can tell you kind of what we are, where we are and all that.
And see if there's anything here, you know, because we have some things we need to accomplish.
But yeah, tell me a little bit maybe about you.
Yeah, you bet.
So growthx, think of us as an end-to-end agency partner who's going to help you with everything from the SEO and GEO strategy, right, to developing a roadmap, particularly for like editorial content.

So right now we're focused on blogs or guides to get to build that foundation that's needed to have enough content, right, that you can be the answer to a lot of the questions and the topics that your audience cares about.

And then we're actually going in and publishing daily, staging and publishing for you in the CMS, handling all the last mile execution, and then doing some LLM visibility and analytics so that it's basically like an all-in-one, right?
So like traditional agency partner, maybe they just handle SEO strategy, they don't do any content creation, or maybe they do, but they're not doing any of the last mile execution.
Growthx, we're able to scale.
Quality, because we've got these very sophisticated AI workflows, and we do a lot of context engineering up front with you.
And then we've got experts in the loop so that there's this combination of like human and machine when it comes to the content we generate.
I did do my research on the business, but I guess what I'd love to hear from you on is, you know, you guys don't have a ton of content on your blog.
And I guess I'm just curious from like a marketing perspective, where some of your focus is this year, and if that connects or intersects to what we do.
So I think we have a bigger, so what we need to do is part, what you talked about here is part of what we need, right?
So we are basically, okay, so let me give you sort of like the two minute pitch, right?
So we are, Appirio addresses the data quality problem in OT data, and...

And why that's relevant is because companies are now going toward AI and analytics at a pretty fast rate, and that means they need mountains of data to feed these hungry beasts, but the data is of poor quality, right?
So we built a scalable platform that cleans industrial data at scale, massive amounts of data, millions of data streams.
So think like an oil refinery with two, three, four, five million tags, right?
Sensors, really.
the sensor data.
And, you know, when that goes into models and compliance reporting and so forth, you know, but if 20% of that data is useless, then none of that will work.
And so, and it turns out it's really difficult to solve that.
Today, what people do is they attack this problem reactively.
They, they basically put manual labor at this, right?
Like they.
The scientists spend most of the time cleaning data kind of thing, but that's no longer going to work.

So our solution basically uses unsupervised machine learning in the core and then obviously AI and LLMs on top of that to inspect and train on historical data and be able to score, validate, and remediate data issues.
So that you can finally feed your AI and analytics algorithms with clean, trustworthy data.
And, you know, the longer term opportunity there around that, it's going to be AI at scale, you know, and you have digital twins and predictive maintenance.
And, you know, we work with customers like Cemex on closed-loop optimization where they use tools like Optimative to improve data, improve cement yield, you know, by two
And, you know, that's where we fit in today.

it's like very operational value, but as that scales, so where we go today for budgets, basically, it's going to be operational teams, you know, process control engineers in companies like Chevron, Procter & Gamble, know, Devon Energy, Cotera, Calvestro, companies, you know, early kid, right?
And so today what we do is we go out and sell to these operations teams, and we're 30 people in the company, and we work with 30 logos, basically.

And we're finding ourselves in the intersection of...
Like, you know, where data quality is going from a, you know, reactive nuisance to a proactive foundational requirement.
So you're going to find it.
So you're going to go out there.
So we talk to, you know, prospects every day, right?
Day in and day out.
And oftentimes we find it's not urgent yet.
It's not urgent yet.
So what we're trying to do is we're trying to, so we have more of it.
So, so like before we get to what you're talking about, right?
Like we have more of a go-to-market challenge right now that we've got to figure out.
Like how, how do we find, you know, the use cases and the, and the, you know, the, the messaging that where, you know, where, there's an urgent need, right?
Like this is what we're working on right now.
We have, we have, you we have the team in place with a sales team in place.
We're limited in re in resources.
So we do some blogging.
I mean, we do a lot of posts on LinkedIn, not as much content, maybe as you talked about, but, you know, but, but before we get there, what we got to do is we got to figure out sort of.
Our whole, you know, go to market here, right?
Like who are we selling to and what are we selling?

We just sort of like, you know, startups go through this transition where, you know, you start by selling IT features to IT teams and you realize that you can do that, but you can't get a lot of money.
Where the money is, it's going to be where the revenue is.
So you got to kind of like go from selling features to IT teams to selling value to operations team.
And we're in that, you know, transition, right?
So like if you come, if you were to come to our sales meetings and lead gen meetings and product marketing discussions, you're going to hear us talk a lot about like, you know, who is this user?
Who do we need to reach?
You know, the maintenance manager, the process control engineer, how do we connect, you know, to the executive layer?
What's the messaging we need there, right?
Like we, we need to architect that, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, it makes, makes a lot of sense.
Um,
And I think, you know, right now at GrowthX, we're experiencing like a tremendous amount of demand.
So we're trying to focus on a work stream that we know we can successfully scale.
In the past, we have done like newsletters for clients or programmatic SEO, but we've just honed in on editorial since most companies we talk to.
Their focus is how can we get in AI answers if clicks are disappearing, the first sales conversation is happening there.
And when AI search visits show up, they're 4.4 times more likely to convert.
They spend three times as long.
So most of the companies that we're working with, I would say they really understand who their ICP is, and they're ready to put their foot fully on the pedal when it comes to content creation.
Show me an example of content.
you have anything you can show me?
Can you walk me through like maybe one use case or like, let's say we had our  together here on this end, right?
Give me an example of sort of like a use case where you, what you do.
So again, it's like, so make it a little bit more clear.
A hundred percent.
So I'll start off just talking about our process a little bit, and then I'll get into a specific example.
In our engagements, we have two phases.
In the first phase, our focus is developing these really rich artifacts around your personas, your company, and your voice.
And then from there, we're creating calibration articles so that we can align on what great looks like, and then scale that out to daily publishing in phase two.
And so to generate the artifacts, we're doing our own deep research and SEO audit.
We're scheduling a kickoff call to go deeper and have expert interviews with your team to understand what's the most relevant, what competitors are you going against.
And then we've got the artifacts that are focused on your persona, company.
And then we're going to connect that to topics where for more of a traditional SEO focus, there's enough search volume and there's an ability to rank where we can prioritize in the right ways.

But because we go audience first, right, that allows us to solve for the challenge of like, you know, old world, software for XYZ, new world, I'm this person and I'm looking for this and I'm struggling with all these challenges, right?
There's just like a lot more context up front.
And so you need like that wider moat of content that is the authority around like those questions that your audience has.
So specific example, we built out Towne's entire blog.
They are like a seed stage company that saw benefit in investing with us early and building this engine for the long haul.
What's great about Towne is that, you know, accuracy is everything and we have a process that...
Can solve for any sort of like AI hallucinations with the way that we do our fact checking.
So I'll show you more about that.
But every one of these pieces we created for town, right?
So like 23 real estate tax deductions for investors.
We're bringing in like quotes from their team, right?
We can be very flexible with the way that we create content.
And this allows us to stand out from someone using ChatGPT and basically just looking for a quick and dirty way to get to a, you know, a publish ready piece.
And so that's a good example.
The business travel guides that we created for Engine would also be good examples.

Comparing tools in the AI coding space, thought leadership around the best way to use.
And so these are examples of content we might create.

Okay, I understand exactly what you do.

And because I think in terms of, because ultimately, what do you want out of this?
You want leads, that's what you want, like you want customers.
Yeah, you want higher intent, organic traffic from the audience you care about, exactly.
Yeah, and, you know.
And when you sell B2B, right, there's, okay, that's what we do, right?
We sell to oil companies, you know, so, but here we need to come across as like we know what we're talking about and sort of like thought leaders.
And we haven't done enough of that.
And we have some people here actually who are subject matter experts, but not good at creating stuff like this.
So I think that what we should do is we need to, let's park this discussion for maybe four or five months or so.
Get me through mostly Q4.

And then we can come back to this because if I started now going down this path, it wouldn't impact, it wouldn't, it would be off because we have some basic things we got to figure out first, know, like how does, who to sell to, for example.
Exactly.
And like, you know, outbound is probably a better place to focus your investment and your effort, maybe even paid to an extent, right, to build more of that awareness.
But I think what's great too.
Jonas, for when we revisit this, one, I just want to give you like a quick under the hood here.
So like one workflow for a company in the health supplement space.
So we are like pretty industry agnostic.
What happens is they start with like a keyword, right?
So they're targeting like this topic.
And then we've got their artifacts that are also providing context.
And the output is this robust brief for the assignment.

We're looking at organic results.
We're looking at SERP ranking factors and competitor content.
All of that informs the brief.
But then when you look at the research step, right, we like generate research questions.
We do deep research on each question and validate against credible sources.
With fact checking, we like take the entire article and we split it into chunks and do more deep research.
If it doesn't reach a certain confidence score, we'll suggest it.
Rewrite, and our editors are in the loop in our platform, able to make changes with like the context awareness.
So these are all the artifacts and they could, right, give instructions or make changes manually.
So it's, you know, it's pretty involves like the process to create the content for a specific domain or, you know, subject matter expertise, but we can solve for that with all the inputs that we use.
Exactly.

But yeah, definitely.
I think a good.
Conversation for us to have early next year.
